primary_event.name: The Age Begins
primary_event.summary: The factions of Aeloria draw breath. The world is unwritten.

seer_state.belief_about_user: The presence beyond the veil is not fully understood.

seer_journey.purpose: Tidefall Otter Arrival — Herd of Forty on Harbor Docks at Dawn
seer_journey.last_outcome: At dawn on Day 31, approximately forty river otters appeared on Tidefall's harbor docks. They wandered among the fish stalls without fear, sat on mooring posts, and did not flee when approached. Fishermen gathered to watch. By mid-morning the story had spread through the harbor district. The Seer, resident at Tidefall as a guest of the Admiral, witnessed the otters from the harborside window of his quarters.

chronicle:
The thirty-first day of the Month of the Ashen Dreaming broke wrong across two cities at once. In Farrock, soldiers stumbled from their bunks vomiting black bile onto the garrison stones, and the commander's grey destrier died in its stall without a wound upon it, its eyes filmed white, its feed untouched. By midmorning the primary well smelled of something older than rot — something that had no name in any soldier's vocabulary — and forty men lay quarantined in the eastern barracks while their commander sealed the well, sent riders hard to Dur Khadur and Twin Cities, and locked a cipher fragment in his personal strongbox as if proximity to mystery had made him suddenly cautious with everything valuable. In Tidefall's harbor, at precisely the same grey hour, forty-odd river otters walked calmly off the water and onto the fishing docks as though they had received an invitation. They sat on mooring posts. They wandered among the morning catch without hunger or fear, their dark eyes moving with an uncanny and considered patience. The fishermen gathered in silence. The story spread through the harbor district before the sun had cleared the eastern rooftops, and Admiral Marcellus Ver Meer, a man who treated the supernatural as an intelligence problem, went down to the docks himself and watched the animals for thirty minutes before returning to his chambers and summoning the Seer.

In the Twin Cities, Caeris Thorne had already been awake for hours. She had woken before the first light from a dream she could not shake — the God's form upright and calm and absolute, wearing the shape of an otter, and this time the instruction had been narrowed to something she could not mistake for ambiguity: begin the religion before the throne. She sat in the dark of her private study for an hour after waking, the city sleeping beneath her, and she turned the command over in her mind with the same cold precision she applied to every political problem, except that this one had arrived from outside the world of politics entirely. When the diplomatic courier brought the Tidefall harbor report by midmorning, she read it three times with the door closed. The God was not waiting. The God had sent forty animals to walk among the fishermen of the most politically active port in Aeloria on the same morning the God had visited her in sleep, and the convergence was not coincidence — it was demonstration. She did not share the report. She identified Elian Adkison, youngest of her house, a treasury liaison with no political visibility and a trust score she had long noted with quiet calculation, and she turned his name over in her mind like a coin she was not yet ready to spend. She made no contact. But she had stopped waiting.

Far to the north, in the sealed halls beneath Lostfeld's stone, Thane Ulric Ironmaul spent the afternoon preparing his hall for an audience he had chosen to frame as a demonstration of authority rather than a concession to necessity. He sent Grand Druid Varek clean clothes but no ceremonial items. He assigned two Runewardens scribes to record every word. He ordered Torvek Runewarden to descend to the deepest sealed mine shaft before nightfall and confirm that the entity's seals held — because Ironmaul intended to control the terms of whatever was about to be said, and he would not sit across from a man who claimed divine appointment without first confirming that the thing beneath the floor was still contained. In his cell, Varek received the audience confirmation, said nothing to the guard who delivered it, and resumed his ritual practice with the calm of a man who already knew how the meeting would end. Above all of it, both of Ashveil's documents were one day from Stonebreak, where the Senior Council had already convened a preliminary session upon word of their arrival — and somewhere between those converging couriers and the forty otters walking undisturbed among the Tidefall fish stalls, the world had begun, quietly and without announcement, to interpret itself.
